speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Walsh, I understand that this is a collage of your family, and of some monuments.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: I wonder if you could tell us who's in each picture, and where it was taken, and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, unfortunately, I don't have too many pictures, or, actually, very few because the Germans and the Nazis, when they destroyed the Jewish people-- or tried to destroy-- they also destroyed our properties, and destroyed-- any pictures or documents that we have had were taken away. Here, I just have, left over, some picture that my brother saved of-- Yitshak had saved a picture of Yakov. And here is, in this picture, you see, is my brother, Yakov.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is, in this picture, about 17 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was taken in Stepan, just about a year or so before he died, or they killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the top picture, in the middle, with the yellow marker, highlighted?

speaker: interviewee
text: The top picture is the names of the military cemetery in Stepan, where all the people that were serving in the [NON-ENGLISH], which is the army that was after and the police, Soviet police, to look up all the Nazis, and to supervise, and help out, the people that lived in the city of Stepan. And these are all the people that died. And the the ones-- the highlight is my brother, Chaim. His name, in Russian, was Ctepan Waks. And the other brother that you saw, Yakov, I don't have the cemetery picture, or any other details about him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture on the right is a monument in Korchivla, where, like I said before, in my story, the people from Stepan and all the surrounding villages were, here, murdered. And a good friend of ours, [INAUDIBLE], put up this monument, and we all pitched in to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the same thing on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --for the expense.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same on the left-hand side, the same thing. This monument is the same monument in Korchivla. The one at the bottom, is in Huta Stepanska. That's where they also killed some of our Jews from Huta Stepanska and surrounding area. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is the entrance gate to the military cemetery where my brothers are buried, in Stepan. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is just a picture when I arrived to-- to Poland. The one on the left-hand side is with-- I am with some friends and our teacher in Krakow. The other picture was taken with my friend, George Kokh, that I was talking about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's the center one, George Kokh. Unfortunately, he passed away about 10 years ago. And the other friend is Shlomo Grinshtain, on the left-- to my left. He lives in Israel. And he's the one I was talking about, I went to school in Lwow with.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Bytom, Poland in 1947. The one at the bottom is in 1947, I was sent to a summer camp. And there, we were with the-- from the Zionist organization Hanoar Hatzioni. And were trained for some people, expert for-- for military training, for Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm here, at the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in the center-- in the center row?

speaker: interviewee
text: Front, in the center. Yeah, front.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this last picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a poem was read at a memorial for all the Jews in Beth Am synagogue. And the-- the gentleman wrote this poem in Yiddish and was translated to-- to English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this last photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph, a cousin of mine, Joseph Fishman, who also survived the Holocaust, and now, unfortunately, died about five years ago. He had this picture. And it was taken, as you see, in 1910, and was mailed to the United States, to one of the relatives. And he gave me a copy of this. And my father is right where you see the arrow, with the beard.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the third one from the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: So the third on from the right--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the rear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and my uncle, Chaim Waks, is this one here. And this--

speaker: interviewer
text: He's on the far right? In the rear--

speaker: interviewee
text: Far right. And all the far right, the lady is my aunt, from Sarny, Shabtai Waks' mother, my cousin that passed away in Israel, about five years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is anybody else in that picture a relative of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are, many of them, but I don't know who it is. This is my cousin, the one I said, Joseph Fishman. He was just a baby--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is being held by his-- the mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is being held-- And he passed away about five years ago. He was 85 years old. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] OK, this is a certificate I received when I was going in school, in Poland, after I finished for a machinist. And I had to pass a government exam. And that's my certificate in the town of Bytom, Silesia, in Poland, upper Silesia in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my wife, and myself, the Huppah. This was taken in Montreal. The wedding took place June the 23rd, 1957, in the Beth Aaron synagogue in Park Extension. And on the left-hand side is a friend of mine, Tom. Tom. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the occasion for this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the wedding of our daughter Anna to Jerry Silverberg. It was taken at-- in 1992. And, to the far right, is Frank Friedman-- our son-in-law, Frank Friedman, and our daughter, Sheila. And next to Sheila, myself, and then my wife, Sonia. And then the bride is Anna. The groom is Jerry Silverberg. Then Yona, my daughter. Yona, the middle daughter, Yona, and her husband Earl Viner. Front, sitting, are my in-laws, Sonia's parents-- Mr. and Mrs. Diamond.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: At no-- what's the name of the shul?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it in Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Toronto, yes. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken just recently, of our granddaughter, Lisa, and she is four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken in Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken here in Toronto, yeah, in-- in our house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture is of our grandson, Jason Silverberg. He's nine months old in here, and it was taken in Toronto, in our house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture is of my brother, Yitshak and his wife, Eva. And the center is my youngest daughter Sheila. She was visiting in Israel, in Ashdod, and it was taken about eight years ago. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is of my niece Jeanette, and her husband Shlomo, and their baby. I took this. They live in Paris, and I took it three years ago, in their house in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose daughter is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my brother's daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yitshak's daughter, the one that lives in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Israel. And that's my niece, second to my left. Looking from the right, is my niece [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sophia, and her son, and her daughter [? Rosalit. ?] And on the-- all the way to my right is my cousin, Talia, and her daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you talking about the one in the white blouse now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture is my eldest niece, Claudia Chaya. She is named after my mother-- alav hasholem-- And their son, Joseph, and Yoni, and her husband Shoni.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where and when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in Israel at bar mitzvah of Yoni's.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 10 years ago. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Israel, when I was in Israel about eight years ago. To my right is Israel, [INAUDIBLE]. My niece's husband, Asha's husband. And my other niece's two girls and myself. That's all. That's [INAUDIBLE] And this picture is, again, was taken in Israel, Ashdod. To my far right, at the rear, is my niece Claudia, myself, then Sophia, my niece, Sophia, and my niece, Asha. To my far right, again, is-- is another niece of mine, Janetta, and my brother in the center, Yitshak and his wife, Eva, and their grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the purpose for this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a family picture, since I visit every so many years, and I go to visit 'em. So we take pictures every time we can have it with--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was about eight years ago. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to introduce, here, my lovely wife, Sonia. And-- and behind us is my daughter, Yona, and our lovely grandson, Colby. Unfortunately, the others couldn't come. But we have pictures of them. And we're happy to be here. And thank you.
